topic: Section 2.3 — Gravity at the Rim: 90% Drop (post-EXP-121 rewrite)
change_summary: EXP-121 integrated: section fully rewritten with dilemma framing (H(r) pressure-column model vs WGS84 curve-fit model), explicit H(r) and g(r) computation at pole/equator/rim, and pole-to-pole symmetry argument. Queue item 102 (also 105 — duplicate).
our_argument_summary: The dome's firmament height H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) drops by a factor of ~10.2 from North Pole (8,537 km) to Antarctic rim (837 km). If gravity arises from aetheric pressure columns (the dome's implicit mechanism), a 10× column reduction should produce ~90% gravity variation. The dome's actual g(r) = 9.7803·(1 + 0.005307·exp(−r/8619)) produces only 0.48% variation, matching real gravity — because 9.7803 is WGS84 equatorial gravity and 0.005307 is approximately the WGS84 gravity flattening constant. Result: either (a) gravity couples to H(r) and the dome predicts a non-existent 10× gradient, or (b) gravity is independent of H(r) and the dome's mechanism is a curve-fit with no physical basis. Both horns falsify the model. Supporting argument: North and South Pole gravity are nearly identical (~9.832 m/s²) on the globe — a symmetry the dome's radial monotonic g(r) cannot reproduce (predicts 9.8322 at r=0 vs 9.7854 at r=20,015).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Not directly applicable — this is a sections-level review. The dome's gravity domain (domain 4 in live-power.txt) publishes the formula g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(-r/λg)) explicitly. H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) is published in 01-home.txt, 04-coordinates.txt, 05-model.txt, 07-ai-context.txt. Both formulas are present in the dome's own text and share the same scale length 8619 km.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The dome's gravity formula values are static in the source text. The claim 'matches WGS84 to 0.05%' is trivially satisfied because the formula IS WGS84 parameters.
data_sources_used: raw-text files only (no API fetches reviewed here — this is a sections-level review)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome can deflect by (1) denying the pressure-column mechanism, (2) invoking an unspecified nonlinear aetheric coupling, or (3) attributing gravity to 'dielectric interaction' decoupled from H(r).
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Minor: the section quotes 'unidirectional dielectric acceleration' as the dome's description, but no such phrase appears in the raw-text. The dome actually says 'Dielectric pressure with aetheric density variation' (raw-text/08-live-power.txt, Domain 4). See holes_found #1.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level review — code tags apply to individual WINs, not prose sections.

holes_found.description: Fabricated/inaccurate quoted attribution. The section puts 'unidirectional dielectric acceleration' in quotation marks attributing the phrase to the dome model: "The dome describes gravity as 'unidirectional dielectric acceleration' arising from aetheric pressure between the firmament and the surface." I searched all raw-text files (01–08) and the older raw-text-v50.6-2026-03-12 snapshot — the phrase does not appear anywhere. The actual dome text reads 'Dielectric pressure with aetheric density variation' (raw-text/08-live-power.txt, Domain 4: Equatorial Gravity). A dome defender who checks the source will accuse us of fabricating a quote, which undermines the entire section and the review's credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the fabricated quote with the actual dome phrasing. Suggested revision: 'The dome describes gravity as "dielectric pressure with aetheric density variation" (Domain 4, live-power dashboard) — a mechanism in which aetheric medium between the firmament and the surface produces the observed downward acceleration.' Alternatively, drop the quotation marks entirely and paraphrase in our own voice without attribution: 'The dome attributes gravity to aetheric pressure between the firmament and surface.'

holes_found.description: Equator distance r ≈ 10,008 km is a linear-interpolation estimate that contradicts the dome's own published city coordinates. The dome's V13 system gives Cairo (lat +30°) r = 8,194 km and Perth (lat −32°) r = 8,727 km (raw-text/04-coordinates.txt). Extrapolating to lat 0° gives r ≈ 8,000–8,500 km, not 10,008 km. 10,008 km is a/2 under a linear 90°→0 / 0°→a/2 / −90°→a mapping, which the V13 Finsler metric does not use. A dome defender pointing at the published city table would say our equator estimate is wrong. The core argument (10× H(r) drop from pole to rim) is not affected — at r=8,500 H is still 3,184 km, 2.7× less than the pole; the pole-to-rim ratio is what matters.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) compute H(r) at the dome's actual equator-like radius (~8,500 km, giving H ≈ 3,184 km, a factor ~2.7 from pole) and drop the 10,008 km claim, or (b) explicitly flag the simplification: 'r ≈ 10,008 km under linear mapping; the V13 Finsler metric gives somewhat smaller r at low latitudes — but the pole-to-rim 10× factor is the structural result.' Option (a) is cleaner and harder to attack.

holes_found.description: Overstated pressure-column premise. The sentence 'In any pressure-column model, gravitational acceleration should scale roughly with the height of the column above you' is too strong. In incompressible fluid columns, pressure scales with h but g is external (independent of h). In atmospheric physics, gravity at altitude varies by the inverse-square law (geometric), not linearly with column height. A defender could say 'pressure columns and gravity are separable — you're assuming a specific coupling that doesn't have to exist.' The section does partially address this in anticipated_objections #4, but the headline framing is vulnerable.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to: 'If aetheric pressure columns cause gravitational acceleration (the dome's stated mechanism), g should scale with column properties — primarily column height or mass — absent fine-tuned cancellation.' Or flip the framing: 'The dome offers no independent parameter that could cancel the 10× H(r) variation down to 0.48%; any mechanism that decouples g from H(r) deprives H(r) of physical meaning.'

holes_found.description: Redundancy with Section 2.8 (Gravity Gradient with Latitude). Both sections argue the dome's exponential profile predicts a large gravity variation that isn't observed. Section 2.8 uses essentially the same g(r) and same λ_g = 8,619 km, and cites the same ~0.5% observed variation. The new Section 2.3 also closes with 'See also Section 2.8' — acknowledging the overlap. EXP-121 already flagged this. A defender can ask: 'Why does the review use TWO sections to make ONE argument? Padding the self-contradictions list to pump a number?' Either merge, or differentiate crisply.
holes_found.recommendation: Suggested differentiation: 2.3 leads with the H(r)↔g(r) STRUCTURAL contradiction (shared scale length, incompatible physics — the 'dilemma' framing is unique to 2.3). 2.8 focuses on LATITUDE-DEPENDENCE (where does gravity actually vary vs predicted), ideally citing specific surveyed gravity values at lat φ and corresponding dome-predicted values at r_dome(φ). This lets 2.3 keep its strong dilemma structure and 2.8 become the empirical-data-vs-dome-prediction pair.

holes_found.description: WGS84 flattening-constant attribution imprecise. The section says '0.005307 is approximately the WGS84 gravity flattening constant (the standard lists 0.00530)'. The precise WGS84 gravity flattening is f* = (g_p − g_e)/g_e = 0.005302 (not 0.00530), and this quantity is a DERIVED parameter, not a raw WGS84 coefficient. Somigliana's normal-gravity formula uses coefficient k = (b·g_p − a·g_e)/(a·g_e) ≈ 0.001931851. The dome's 0.005307 is closer to the old International Gravity Formula 1967 coefficient 0.0052884. The larger point — 'the dome's number matches a known globe-derived coefficient' — stands, but the specific attribution can be nitpicked.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe as: 'The coefficient 0.005307 closely tracks the International Gravity Formula 1967 value 0.0052884 and the WGS84 gravity-flattening f* ≈ 0.005302 — standard geodesy constants established decades before the dome model.' This is harder to attack on specifics.

holes_found.description: TLDR phrasing: 'drops by a factor of 10 from pole to rim, which should produce dramatic gravity variation if gravity arises from aetheric pressure. The dome's actual gravity formula produces only 0.5% variation — because it's the WGS84 standard in disguise.' A non-science reader parsing the heading '90% Drop' alongside 'the dome's actual gravity formula produces only 0.5% variation' could get confused about who predicts what. The section is arguing that the dome's OWN PHYSICS predicts a 90% drop that its OWN FORMULA doesn't deliver — that nuance is easy to lose.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider: 'The dome's firmament height H(r) drops 10× from pole to rim. If gravity arises from aetheric pressure (as the dome claims), this should produce ~90% gravity variation. The dome's actual published g(r) formula gives only 0.5% — because it's really the WGS84 globe standard in disguise. The dome's own two core equations contradict each other.' Clearer attribution of 'predicted by mechanism' vs 'delivered by formula'.

holes_found.description: Pole-to-pole symmetry argument would be stronger with a specific measurement citation. The section says 'North Pole and South Pole gravity are nearly identical (~9.832 m/s² each)' — true, but a dome defender could ask 'source?' Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station gravity measurements are publicly available (Antarctic Geophysics Database, Scripps); specifically g at geodetic South Pole ≈ 9.8322 m/s² at sea level (with free-air reduction applied). Adding this number, with a source, upgrades the argument from assertion to citation-backed.
holes_found.recommendation: Append to the globe-comparison paragraph: 'At Amundsen-Scott Station (geodetic South Pole), gravity measures g ≈ 9.832 m/s² (sea-level reduced; Scripps Antarctic Geophysics Database). The dome's g(r=20,015) = 9.7854 m/s². This is a direct ~0.5% discrepancy at a specific coordinate — 10× larger than the dome's stated 0.05% accuracy claim for WIN-011.'

stronger_arguments.description: Historical precedence: the WGS84 parameters 9.7803 and 0.005302 were published in 1984 (and their IGF-67 precursors in 1967) — decades before the dome model. The dome's 9.7803 and 0.005307 are almost certainly ADOPTED from published WGS84/IGF tables, not derived from dome geometry. This reframes 'curve-fit' as 'value adoption,' which is a stronger accusation because it implies the formula never attempts a derivation at all.

GRACE/GRACE-FO satellite gravimetry: published gravity anomaly maps (2002–present) show global gravity variation at the ±0.001 m/s² level (anomalies up to ~0.04 m/s² in specific features). There is no large-scale radial decay from any pole. An exponential g(r) centered on the North Pole would produce a detectable anomaly pattern that GRACE does not see. This is stronger than pointing at isolated pole measurements.
sources: Tapley et al. 2019 Nat. Climate Change (GRACE/GRACE-FO overview)

stronger_arguments.description: V21 monitoring check: monitor.py (Domain 4, Equatorial Gravity) treats the formula output as the prediction and compares against a single equatorial benchmark value. Per WIN-011 code analysis (monitoring=hardcoded, derives_from_dome=false), the code does NOT perform a radial-gravity test. The code's structure itself acknowledges that g(r) at non-equatorial r isn't being validated. This is a concession built into the dome's own automation.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome's g(r) formula is a credible, parsimonious three-parameter fit that reproduces WGS84 equatorial-to-polar gravity variation across latitudes with ~0.05% accuracy. The author has deliberately chosen to share the scale length λ_g between H(r) and g(r), signaling an intent to unify firmament geometry with gravity. The aesthetic is coherent: one scale length, two related functions. That's more structural commitment than most flat-earth models make.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The unification is superficial. The shared λ_g appears only in a 0.48% correction term on top of a WGS84-derived base; the 'unification' has almost no numerical consequence. Meanwhile the H(r) function's 10× variation would completely dominate any mechanism that actually coupled firmament geometry to gravity. The dome has purchased visual consistency (same exponential) at the cost of physical coherence (the exponential does opposite things in the two formulas). You cannot have both the dome's stated pressure-based gravity mechanism AND a firmament that falls by a factor of 10 without observable consequences.

deeper_analysis:
The section is structurally strong — the dilemma framing is legitimate and the quantitative argument (10× H drop vs 0.48% g variation) is correct. The main vulnerabilities are attribution accuracy (hole #1, the 'unidirectional dielectric acceleration' fabrication is the single most serious flaw — it hands a defender a concrete gotcha) and the equator-distance simplification (hole #2, easy to verify against the dome's own city table). Holes #3 and #4 are framing/structural. Holes #5–7 are polish.

If I had one change to make before publication, it would be fixing hole #1. A made-up quote is the kind of thing that gets pasted into takedown replies and quoted against us. The EXP-121 author picked a phrasing that SOUNDS like it could be from the dome, but actually isn't. Replace with the verbatim 'Dielectric pressure with aetheric density variation' from live-power.txt Domain 4 and the argument becomes bulletproof on that point.

On the other side of the argument (per user preference): a thoughtful dome defender's strongest counter is probably 'you're attacking a mechanism I never endorsed.' If the dome's gravity is purely phenomenological — a curve fit to observed values without a physical mechanism — then the H(r) contradiction dissolves because H(r) was never claimed to drive gravity. This collapses horn A of our dilemma. BUT this then walks the dome into horn B (no physics, just a curve fit), and since the dome's marketing is built around 'aetheric physics,' giving up the mechanism is itself a concession. So the dilemma survives even if the defender retreats to 'it's just a fit.'

One more counter worth noting: a defender could claim that dome gravity IS a WGS84-like phenomenon because the dome's aetheric medium just happens to organize in the same pattern as the WGS84 oblate spheroid's gravity field. This is non-falsifiable: any observed gravity pattern can be post-hoc attributed to 'aetheric self-organization.' We should consider flagging this as the canonical escape hatch. The anticipated_objections list doesn't capture this version cleanly.

citations_verified: WGS84 equatorial gravity g_e = 9.7803253359 m/s² (NIMA 1984) — matches section's 9.7803
WGS84 polar gravity g_p = 9.8321849378 m/s² — matches section's pole claim
Dome formula g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(-r/λg)) — verified in raw-text/08-live-power.txt
Dome formula H(r) = 8537×exp(-r/8619) — verified in raw-text/01-home.txt, 04-coordinates.txt, 05-model.txt
H(r=0) = 8537, H(r=10008) ≈ 2673, H(r=20015) ≈ 837 — computationally verified (section's numbers correct)
g(r=0) = 9.8322, g(r=10008) = 9.7966, g(r=20015) = 9.7854 — computationally verified (section's numbers correct)
citations_failed: Dome quote 'unidirectional dielectric acceleration' — NOT FOUND in any raw-text file. Actual text: 'Dielectric pressure with aetheric density variation' (08-live-power.txt). This is a fabricated/paraphrased quotation. MAJOR.
citations_unchecked: WGS84 'gravity flattening constant 0.00530' — the section's attribution is loose. Actual WGS84 gravity flattening f* ≈ 0.005302. The 0.00530 figure is a rounded form but the section implies it's a raw WGS84 coefficient, which is imprecise.
Antarctic gravity at geographic South Pole ~9.832 m/s² — asserted but not cited. Suggest adding Scripps/NOAA/IGS reference.
